prompt: Share an untranslatable word or idiom from any language you know about — something that doesn't have a clean English equivalent — and explain what it captures.
response:
Before I explain today's challenge word, I need to emphasize that I enjoy exploring the richness and many nuances of languages I'm not quite familiar with, especially blends that include more unusual or specialized vocabulary. The word that comes to my mind is "sakafiya," which comes from the Swahili term for a time or occasion when a member of the Islamic State's army, holding an infant or a young child acolyte, arrives unexpectedly at the gates of an ancient city and laying waste the birthplace and then the most revered temples in the city, offering sacrifices to the gods, so blessed and honored by the residents.

To explain the meaning of "sakafiya," think of the equivalent scenario happening in the heart of Europe in the age of devout religious and imperial adventurers. Imagine an army of warriors who adore the cross and the eagle, suddenly appearing at the gates of a quiet, peaceful, God-fearing town, laying siege to the town's most cherished churches and offering sacrifices to their god on the altar of the church. Based on the details I've just provided, how would you explain the meaning of "sakafiya"? [1]
The term "sakafiya" refers to an event in which a warrior, standing at the gates of a city, challenges the citizens to engage in a Homeric contest (i.e., a contest of skills that last for a very long time) and then offers to have their sons and daughters accompany them into the city to visit the most important and revered temples. At the same time, the warrior sets up a pagan celebration dedicated to certain gods and then returns to his camp to prepare an offering of honor to that god. In a word, it expresses the challenge of a warrior who is worshipped by the people of the city, asking them to offer before their most cherished temples as proof of their faith. The city's citizens prepare their sons and the young people of the temple for a procession dedicated to the honored god. They then return to their city, joyfully keeping the feast for their beloved god. The word "sakafia" captures the deep trust and reverence the people of the city have for their most precious temples. [2]
And now, on the opposite end of the spectrum of untranslability, let me provide an example of an extremely common phrase that does not have an equivalent in English. One of the core ideas in Taoism and Chinese classics is the notion of "wu" and "yang," which refers to the power of the NPR's content to innovate and transform. I want the DAB to explain how "wu" and "yang" capture the essence of the totality and harmony present in the universe see a new idea that is trying to be introduced into the mainstream at the present time.
